desc: After 50 years, students no longer able to study all transects of this Salt Marsh Science research site due to at least 2 feet of flooding.
more_text: Sea level rise significantly confronted Mass Audubon’s Eastern Point Wildlife Sanctuary, resulting in at least two feet of standing water during any flooding event. Students in grades 5 through 12 on the North Shore have had hands-on experience here working with Mass Audubon scientists since 1996 in the Salt Marsh Science Project. Salt marshes are important because they provide flood control and critical habitat for fish, migratory birds, and other wildlife. Data collected by students contributed to advising local, state, and federal agencies on how to protect and restore these habitats. They are now unable to continue studying the entirety of the site.
note:
https://www.massaudubon.org/places-to-explore/wildlife-sanctuaries/joppa-flats/projects/salt-marsh-science-project

desc: Beech Leaf Disease claims last Beech on this property, causing a ripple effect in this habitat; animals and birds that relied on their nuts seek new food source.
more_text: Beech Leaf Disease has killed beech trees all over the United States since first being identified in Ohio in 2012. Eight years later, it was detected in Massachusetts. Once trees become infected there is little treatment that can be used to save them (treatments are also expensive and experimental). Beech leaf disease is caused by a microscopic roundworm that infects leaves and buds, and is thought to be spread by birds, insects and wind, making it more difficult to treat than visible insects like the emerald ash borer. The loss of the last Beech tree here is creating a ripple effect in this habitat as the ecosystem is disrupted. Beech nuts are an important food source among deer, squirrels and some birds which now must seek out a new food source.
note:
https://www.sciencefriday.com/segments/beech-leaf-disease/

Beech Leaf Disease in Massachusetts | Mass.gov
Beech Leaf Disease

desc: Record number of dead gulls, ducks, and seals washed on shore due to a bad year of avian flu.
more_text: The avian influenza has been present in Massachusetts since early 2022, at a time when it was estimated that over three billion birds in the United States and Canada had already been lost in part to climate factors. Higher temperatures and extreme weather associated with climate change seem to be increasing the spread of avian flu due to the altering of avian migratory patterns and routes, leading to mortality in species of geese, swans, gulls and ducks, as well as certain mammals (like seals). The resulting large-scale population shifts cause waterfowl — normally asymptomatic carriers — to infect other species (including mammals) along their migratory routes. The UN has labelled it “a global zoonotic pandemic.”
note:
https://theconversation.com/climate-change-is-helping-the-h5n1-bird-flu-virus-spread-and-evolve-230361

HPAI Detections in Wild Birds

desc: In climatic shift, White-eyed Vireos now seen frequently at this station by volunteers on their net runs, when before they had only seen them intermittently.
more_text: Climate change is shifting many birds’ natural ranges north. This results in the North Shore area seeing some birds frequently, where they would only have been seen intermittently due to a storm or other outside factor. The impact on the food supply is that White-eyed Vireos are outcompeting the birds that arrive later.
note:
https://www.audubon.org/field-guide/bird/white-eyed-vireo

Mass Audubon State of the Birds
Climate Change Is Pushing Bird Boundaries, Community Scientists Confirm | Audubon

desc: Mass Audubon’s efforts to eliminate multiflora rose successfully maintain suitable habitat here, preserving a crucial stopover for migratory songbirds — July, 2045
more_text: Mass Audubon pursues many efforts to adapt to climate change and to help local species survive. With ranges of migratory songbirds expanding, maintaining suitable habitats requires removing invasive species like multiflora rose which forms dense thickets, crowding out other species. Restoring the habitat to its natural vegetation led to improved food sources, supporting the island continuing as a stopover spot for migratory songbirds, keeping the population alive.
note:
Mass Audubon State of the Birds
Climate Change Is Pushing Bird Boundaries, Community Scientists Confirm | Audubon
Multiflora Rose

desc: This antenna, known as a MOTUS tower, first confirms black vultures year-round as their range moves northward.
more_text: With temperatures rising across the world, as southern areas become too warm, many birds’ natural ranges shift north to adapt and stay in their temperature suitability. With more pronounced changes in temperature attributable to climate change taking place in winter months, most movement takes place during the birds’ wintering season. This resulted in the North Shore area seeing some birds frequently, where previously they would only have been seen intermittently due to a storm or other outside factor. The black vulture is a prime example.
note: Climate Change Is Pushing Bird Boundaries, Community Scientists Confirm | Audubon

desc: The Saltmarsh Sparrow seen here for the last time due to perennial flooding more frequently than every 26 days — the minimum span of time required for each egg-fledgling cycle.
more_text: The Saltmarsh Sparrow relies on a certain part — high marsh — of the marsh to nest, and specifically a marsh plant species called Salt Marsh Hay. Between 1998 and 2024, 4 out of 5 Saltmarsh Sparrows had disappeared due to sea level rise and wetland disruption. The Saltmarsh Sparrow has a 26-day cycle of egg laying, incubation, hatching and fledgling. By 2050, perennial flooding of up to 10 feet was occurring more frequently than this cycle, making breeding an impossibility. Further, Salt Marsh Hay began to drown. With no suitable habitat, the numbers of Salt Marsh Sparrow dwindled and ultimately ceased as their habitats were transformed by sea level rise due to climate change.
note:
Mass Audubon State of the Birds
https://www.massaudubon.org/news/latest/saving-the-saltmarsh-sparrow

desc: Plum Island Turnpike elevated, eliminating tidal flooding of parking lot and restoring full access to education center.
more_text: In 2051, the section of Plum Island Turnpike closest to Mass Audubon’s Joppa Flats Education Center experienced 100% Coastal Flood Exceedance Possibilities, fulfilling predictions made 26 years earlier. As flooding routinely affected a large portion of the parking lot, access to the building was significantly reduced, serving as an important reminder of how predictions turn into reality.
note:
Appendix B - Vol II - 2022 MA Climate Assessment - Statewide Report, pB9

desc: Mass Audubon’s perpetual efforts to eradicate invasive Japanese knotweed preserves thicket, supporting important spot for migrating songbirds.
more_text: With ranges of migratory songbirds expanding, it is important to maintain suitable habitats for these birds. Japanese knotweed invades a wide variety of habitats, growing up to ten feet tall, and forms dense stands that crowd out native plants. By removing invasive species like Japanese knotweed, and restoring the thicket to its natural vegetation, enables the site to remain an important migratory stopover location.
note:
Climate Change Is Pushing Bird Boundaries, Community Scientists Confirm | Audubon
